summary: Gary Cornell argues that the ubiquity of calculators in math education has eliminated students' need to develop order-of-magnitude estimation skills, which were naturally required when using slide rules. He and Cathy O'Neil agree that math curricula should be restructured around probability, statistics, and simplified calculus to be more practical and help students identify statistical misinformation. Cornell proposes either bringing back slide rules or implementing a 'significant digits only' calculator mode to force students to think about scale. The underlying concern is that calculator precision creates false confidence in exact answers when real-world problems rarely have or need 14 digits of accuracy.
key_points: Slide rules forced students to continuously practice order-of-magnitude calculations because they only display significant digits, not trailing zeros
Modern calculators have made Fermi estimation an 'add-on rather than a baseline skill' which is harmful to scientific thinking
A unified probability-statistics-calculus curriculum would be more practical and help students identify statistical misinformation
High-precision calculator displays create a false belief that real-world problems have or need 14 digits of accuracy
A simple 'significant digits only' calculator mode could restore the educational benefits of estimation without eliminating calculators entirely
money_quotes: Calculators have made skill at doing order of magnitude calculations an add-on rather than a base line skill and that is a really bad thing.
Students would hopefully learn a skill that is essential nowadays: how to separate out statistically good information from the large amount of statistical crap that is out there.
Using a slide rule requires the user to keep track of the 'order of magnitude' of the answers—because slide rules only give you four or so significant digits.
When you see a high precision result in the real world, it is likely to be totally bogus when examined under the hood.
Nothing they are likely to encounter in their work (and certainly not in their lives) will ever need (or even have) 14 digits of accuracy.
key_insight: The shift from slide rules to calculators inadvertently eliminated the constant practice of order-of-magnitude thinking that is essential for detecting bogus precision in real-world claims.
